goal: Refactor sync to use git cat-file --batch — fold N-fork hot paths into one long-lived subprocess; cuts local sync work from 4.7s to 90ms

summary.title: Sync perf: collapse N-fork hot paths via git cat-file --batch
summary.what: Adds a long-lived git cat-file --batch subprocess wrapper (gitops.CatFileBatch, mutex-protected) plus bulk helpers (LogChainTrees, RevListSet, NotesListEntries, CommitDates, CommitTreeHashes). Rewrites three hot paths: (1) storage.ReadActorLogEventsFromRef now does 1 LogChainTrees + 1 batch session per actor instead of 4 forks per chain commit; (2) engine.scanMainNotes does 1 RevListSet + 1 batch session + 1 CommitDates instead of fan-out N×3 forks; (3) engine.merge.Pin auto-sweep does 1 CommitTreeHashes instead of N CommitTreeHash forks. Chronological-replay invariant in scanMainNotes (sort by date, apply sequentially) preserved. The deterministic dedup order in collectAllEvents is also preserved.
summary.why: Profile of the no-fetch sync path showed 73 git child-process invocations after the PR-#28 fan-out work, with 50 of those being CommitTreeHash forks for the auto-pin sweep and ~16 being per-noted-commit forks. cat-file --batch is the canonical pattern git itself uses internally and that git-lfs / git-filter-repo / git-machete adopt; one long-lived process answering N piped queries cuts per-object cost from a ~20ms fork to ~50µs. Local sync work drops from ~4.7s (post-fan-out) to ~90ms — about 66x faster vs. the rc5 baseline. Total wall time goes from 4.85s to 3.67s; the remaining 3.0s is the actual SSH fetch and is network-bound.
summary.user_goal: Make mainline sync feel snappy enough that embedding it in seal --submit, future hooks, or large-repo workflows does not stack visible latency.

decisions.rationale: Older git versions still in the wild on dev machines; --batch is the lowest-common-denominator. We do not need --batch-command features (mixing object types in one stream).

decisions.chose: Serialise all Reads through one mutex
decisions.rationale: The wire protocol is request-response over a single stdin/stdout pipe; concurrent Read calls would interleave bytes and corrupt parsing. Mutex makes the wrapper safe to share across goroutines without forcing per-goroutine subprocess startup.

decisions.point: Replace per-commit merge-base --is-ancestor with one rev-list set
decisions.rationale: For a 50k-commit main this is ~150ms and constant in note count; per-commit checks would be 20ms × N. Memory cost is one SHA per commit (~40 bytes × 50k = 2MB), negligible.

decisions.point: Note-blob direct read vs notes show per commit
decisions.chose: NotesListEntries returns (note-blob, commit) pairs; batch-read by note-blob sha
decisions.rationale: git notes show <commit> internally resolves the path on the notes ref every time. Reading the note-blob sha directly skips that resolution and works inside cat-file --batch as a primary key, not a path-lookup.

For our reachability check we want ALL ancestors, including those brought in by merge commits second-parent side. --first-parent would silently miss them and let manually-pinned never-merged commits leak through the eligibility filter.

decisions.chose: Each actor goroutine opens its own batch process
decisions.rationale: Sharing one batch via mutex would serialise actor reads. For our scale (2 actors) the difference is negligible, but at 10+ actors the parallel fan-out matters; per-goroutine subprocess is cheap given each batch session amortises N queries.

rejected.reason: We always want the body for our use cases (event.json, note bodies).
Hash-based caching would be safe but git already keeps recently-touched objects in OS page cache, so the win is marginal until the working set exceeds RAM.

risks: Long-lived subprocess can leak if Close is missed on an early-return path. Mitigated by defer batch.Close() at every call site and the Close idempotency contract (TestCatFileBatch_CloseIdempotent).

If the batch process crashes mid-stream, subsequent Reads error and the caller propagates. We do not auto-restart; for sync paths a restart attempt is unlikely to help and risks masking a real corruption.
On Windows the named-pipe semantics for stdin/stdout may differ subtly from Unix; we have no Windows CI yet, this would surface there first if anywhere.
followups: Fix the pre-existing TestPropertyRevertNoteShape / TestPropertyReconcileIdempotent flakes — second-precision date sort needs a topological-order tiebreaker. Identified during this PR but split out to keep the perf change focused.
Profile a 10-actor / 1000-event repo to confirm the win scales linearly past the dogfood scale.
Make syncWorkers tunable via [sync] workers for very large repos, if anyone reports actor-log walk being the bottleneck on huge histories.

architectural_claims: gitops layer now exposes a long-lived cat-file --batch wrapper plus bulk helpers (rev-list, log --no-walk, notes-list-entries) that fold N forks into 1.
Sync local git work is bounded by ~6 git invocations regardless of note count or actor count; remaining wall time is the SSH fetch.
Auto-pin sweep no longer issues per-main-commit forks; one log --no-walk fetches all tree hashes.
behavioral_changes: mainline sync local-only wall time drops from ~4.7s (post-PR-#28) to ~90ms on this repo; total including fetch from 4.85s to 3.67s.
Sync now opens git cat-file --batch subprocesses (one per actor log + one for scanMainNotes); each is closed before sync returns.
